other_observers: I was alone when watching the bird at this time.; Bird was reported here about a week ago by someone from out of state, and has been seen by many birders in the intervening days.
specific_location: Along the Devils Kitchen Trail, about 250 meters from the parking lot, just inside Colorado National Monument.
description: The bird was a sparrow, about the size and shape of a Chipping Sparrow, with a notably long tail that had a shallow fork. It had a small, conical bill. Its face was black on a grayish background that covered the head, the sides of the underparts, and the rump. The back was brown; I could not see if it was streaked, and I did not see the wings well. The center of the belly was the lightest part of the underparts, nearly white. The bill was bright pink, contrasting with the black face. The border between black face and gray head was kind of diffuse rather than crisp.Behaviors: The bird would tee up and sing from exposed perches at the top of short juniper or single-leaf ash trees for several minutes at a time, then fly to another perch and sing some more.Call: The bird sang frequently. It had two songtypes, like most Black-chinned Sparrows. Both were accelerating series that started with slow, clear, slurred whistles and ended with a toneless buzz with the elements repeated way too fast to count. The song is extremely distinctive; only a few other birds in North America sing accelerating trills (notable Field Sparrow, Rufous-winged Sparrow, and Wrentit), and none of them start musical and end with a mechanical buzz.Plumage: alternate
